did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a market-approved convolutional neural network (CNN), specifically Moleanalyzer Pro (based on a modified Inception_v4 architecture), to classify pigmented and non-pigmented skin lesions (including skin cancers and benign lesions) from dermoscopic images. The CNN's performance (sensitivity, specificity, AUC) was obtained and compared against dermatologists' diagnostic decisions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a two-level reader study comparing a market-approved convolutional neural network (Moleanalyzer Pro) against 96 dermatologists. It includes original experimental methods, data collection from 100 cases plus external datasets (MSK-1, ISIC-2018), statistical analysis, and new results regarding sensitivity, specificity, and AUC, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Man against machine reloaded: performance of a market-approved convolutional neural network in classifying a broad spectrum of skin lesions in comparison with 96 dermatologists working under less artificial conditions
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of a market-approved convolutional neural network (Moleanalyzer Pro) in classifying a broad spectrum of pigmented and non-pigmented skin lesions compared to 96 dermatologists. The CNN was tested on 100 manually selected cases and two external datasets (MSK-1 and ISIC-2018), with its sensitivity, specificity, and AUC compared against dermatologists' management decisions and diagnostic classifications. Dermatologists participated in a two-level reader study, providing decisions based on dermoscopy alone (Level I) or combined with clinical images and textual information (Level II). The CNN achieved a sensitivity of 95.0% and specificity of 76.7%, while dermatologists' sensitivity significantly improved to 94.1% with additional clinical context. The study concluded that under less artificial conditions, the CNN and most dermatologists performed at a similar level, though more experienced dermatologists frequently surpassed the CNN's performance.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the 100-case test set and external datasets; CNN achieved 100% correct classification in malignant lesions.

Included in the 100-case test set; CNN achieved 70% correct classification in benign lesions.

Included in the 100-case test set; CNN achieved 50% correct classification in benign lesions.

Broad category used in the study design; 100 cases included pigmented/non-pigmented skin cancers and benign lesions.
Broad category used in the study design; included nevi, angioma/angiokeratoma, seborrheic keratoses, dermatofibroma, and solar lentigo.

all_task_types.target: Dichotomous diagnosis (malignant/premalignant vs. benign) and management decisions (treatment/excision, no action, follow-up examination) for skin lesions.
all_task_types.details: The CNN (Moleanalyzer Pro) performs binary classification of pigmented and non-pigmented skin lesions into malignant/premalignant or benign categories using a malignancy score cutoff (>0.5). It also classifies lesions into specific diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma, BCC, SCC, nevi, seborrheic keratosis) to calculate sensitivity and specificity. The primary endpoints are sensitivity and specificity of this dichotomous classification compared to dermatologists' management decisions.

datasets_used.name: Internal Test Set (100 cases)
datasets_used.description: A manually selected dataset of 100 cases including pigmented/non-pigmented and melanocytic/non-melanocytic skin lesions (malignant, premalignant, and benign) from different body sites. Images originated from a convenience sample collected between 2014 and 2019 by the authors. Each case included clinical close-up images, dermoscopic images, textual case information, and histopathological or follow-up data.

per_class.malignant: Included (specific counts not reported, but 75% of benign lesions had equivocal histopathology)
per_class.benign: Included (specific counts not reported, but 25% of benign lesions had unremarkable follow-up data)

use.testing: Used as the primary test set for the two-level reader study comparing 96 dermatologists against the CNN (Moleanalyzer Pro).
use.other: Used to determine the CNN's specificity cutoff by applying it to a 400-image validation set (mentioned in methods) before applying to this test set.

datasets_used.name: MSK-1 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available external dataset containing 1100 dermoscopic images, used to confirm the generalizability of the CNN results.
classes_used: Broad spectrum of skin lesions (specific class distribution not reported)

use.testing: Used for external testing of the CNN's performance to assess generalizability.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2018 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available external dataset containing 1511 dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2018 challenge, used to confirm the generalizability of the CNN results.
classes_used: Broad spectrum of skin lesions (specific class distribution not reported)

use.testing: Used for external testing of the CNN's performance to assess generalizability.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide uncertainty estimates for sensitivity, specificity, and AUC of the CNN and dermatologists' performance metrics.
To compare the CNN's sensitivity (with fixed specificity) with the average sensitivity of dermatologists.
To test for heterogeneity in diagnostic performance between dermatologists with different levels of experience (beginners, skilled, experts).
To perform post hoc pairwise comparisons between experience levels adjusted for multiple comparisons following the Kruskal-Wallis test.
To test for changes in dermatologists' diagnostic performance after receiving Level I or Level II information.
To determine statistical significance for all analyses due to the observational nature of the investigation.

hypothesis_testing.test: Two-sided one-sample t-test, Non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis test, Dunn-Nemenyi procedure, Wilcoxon paired signed-rank test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in sensitivity between the CNN and the average sensitivity of dermatologists; there is no heterogeneity in diagnostic performance between dermatologists with different levels of experience; there is no change in dermatologists' diagnostic performance after receiving additional information.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in sensitivity between the CNN and the average sensitivity of dermatologists; there is heterogeneity in diagnostic performance between dermatologists with different levels of experience; there is a change in dermatologists' diagnostic performance after receiving additional information.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined at the P < 0.05 level. The study reported significant improvements in dermatologists' sensitivity with additional clinical context (P < 0.001) and significant differences in performance based on experience level (P < 0.001 for accuracy, P = 0.005 for specificity). The CNN's sensitivity was found to be similar to dermatologists' when specificity was fixed (P = 0.1), but significantly lower in Level I management decisions (P < 0.001).

Internal Test Set (100 cases).notes: When fixing CNN specificity at dermatologists' mean level II management specificity (80.4%), CNN sensitivity was 95.0% vs dermatologists' 94.1% (P=0.1). Dermatologists were outperformed by CNN in level I management decisions (P<0.001) and level I/II diagnostic decisions (P<0.001). More experienced dermatologists frequently surpassed CNN performance.

MSK-1 Dataset (1100 images).notes: CNN attained almost identical performance compared to the internal test set. Pairwise comparison of ROC AUCs with other datasets revealed no significant differences (all P > 0.527).

ISIC-2018 Challenge Dataset (1511 images.notes: CNN showed lower sensitivity but higher specificity compared to internal test set. Pairwise comparison of ROC AUCs with other datasets revealed no significant differences (all P > 0.527).

architecture_used.pretraining: Specifically trained by dermoscopic images; originally developed by a cooperative consortium of industry and academia.

training_details.training_data_source: Dermoscopic images collected from multiple sources around the world; no overlap between training, validation, and testing datasets.
training_details.training_method: Supervised deep learning (end-to-end approach using raw image data and corresponding diagnostic labels).

classification_or_output_head.cutoff: A priori malignancy cutoff at >0.5 for dichotomous classification (malignant/premalignant vs. benign).

conclusions: Under less artificial conditions and in a broader spectrum of diagnoses, the market-approved convolutional neural network (Moleanalyzer Pro) and most dermatologists performed at a similar level.

When the CNN's specificity was fixed to match the mean specificity of dermatologists' management decisions with additional clinical context (Level II), the CNN's sensitivity (95.0%) was almost equal to that of human raters (94.1%), with no statistically significant difference (P = 0.1).
Dermatologists were outperformed by the CNN in their Level I management decisions (dermoscopy only) and in both Level I and Level II diagnostic decisions.
More experienced dermatologists frequently surpassed the CNN's performance.

Dermatologists are trained to integrate information from a range of sources (e.g., patient risk profile, anamnestic data), rendering comparative studies that rely solely on a single case image inadequate for evaluating real-world clinical performance.

The CNN demonstrated generalizability, attaining almost identical performance on an external dataset (MSK-1) and comparable performance on another external dataset (ISIC-2018), with no significant differences in ROC AUCs across the three datasets.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that current deep learning algorithms lack interpretability, preventing the identification of specific causes for false classifications and hindering targeted improvements.
The test set was limited to 100 lesions to ensure study feasibility, resulting in few cases for certain diagnoses; therefore, CNN results for specific diagnoses should be interpreted with caution.

The compiled test set excluded certain benign (e.g., viral warts), malignant (e.g., Merkel cell carcinoma), and inflammatory skin lesions, limiting the generalizability of results to a broad prospective patient population.
The CNN was mostly trained on dermoscopic images of patients with a Caucasian genetic background, raising concerns about its performance in populations with non-white skin types.

Previous comparative studies were criticized for highly artificial settings where dermatologists received only single images; this study mitigated that by using a store-and-forward tele-dermatology setting with clinical images and textual metadata.
The CNN's specificity cutoff was determined using a separate 400-image validation set before being applied to the test set, which may introduce bias if the validation set is not representative.

Dermatologists' performance significantly improved with additional clinical context (Level II), highlighting that comparative studies relying solely on dermoscopy may inadequately evaluate real-world clinical performance where multimodal information is integrated.
